Quick Facts
  • Parents are Joe Marinko and Teresa McShane
  • Brothers are John Marinko and Zac Boydstun; Sisters are Joy Harold, Tina Bishop and Valerir Powell. J.Dee is the youngest of the family
  • The happiest moment of his life was the birth of his sons, Kash (2008) and Liam (2011)
  • The person J. Dee admires most is his mother, Teresa, because, “She has fought and beat cancer, and was with me through my fight with cancer.”

  • DID YOU KNOW
  • J.Dee is below the knee amputee on his right leg
  • After being diagnosed with synovial sarcoma, J. Dee was told there was a 75 percent chance of the cancer coming back if he did have the affected parts of his leg removed.
  • Played two years of football at Panhandle State University

Quick Facts
  • SFC Elizabeth Marks joined the Army in 2008, shortly after her 17th birthday, continuing a family legacy of military service

  • While on assignment in Iraq as a combat medic, she sustained bilateral hip injuries

  • It wasn’t until 2012, almost two years after her injury, while recovering and trying to be found “fit for duty,” that she discovered an unrealized gift for competitive swimming
  • Six months later she was accepted into the US ARMY World Class Athlete Program (WCAP) and found “fit for duty” as a 68w Combat Medic
  • Since then she has continued to participate and push herself to excel in a sport she has grown to love
  • Daughter of James Marks, who also served and is her hero
  • Married to Mason Heibel
  • 2016 ESPY Pat Tillman Award Recipient
  • 2017 Inductee: Army Woman’s Hall of Fame
Paralympic Experience
  • 3-time Paralympian; 10-time Paralympic medalist (2 gold, 6 silver, 2 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Paris 2024, silver (100m Backstroke S6 - Women, 200m Individual Medley SM6 - Women, 4 x 50m Freestyle Relay 20 Points - Mixed, 4 x 50m Medley Relay 20 Points - Mixed, 50m Freestyle S6 - Women), 4th (100m Freestyle S7 - Women), 7th (50m Butterfly S6 - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, gold (100m Backstroke S6 - Women), silver (50m Freestyle S6 - Women), bronze (50m Butterfly S6 - Women), 4th (200m Individual Medley SM6 - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, gold (100m Breaststroke SB7 - Women), bronze (4 x 100m Medley Relay 34 Points - Women), 8th (100m Backstroke S8 - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2023 – bronze (200m Individual Medley SM6 - Women, 50m Butterfly S6 - Women), 4th (50m Freestyle S6 - Women), 5th (100m Backstroke S6 - Women)
  • Years of participation: 100m Backstroke S6 - Women 2023; 200m Individual Medley SM6 - Women 2023; 50m Butterfly S6 - Women 2022, 2023; 50m Freestyle S6 - Women 2022, 2023; 100m Freestyle S6 - Women 2022; 4 x 50m Medley Relay 20 Points - Mixed 2022; 100m Backstroke S7 - Women 2019; 100m Breaststroke SB6 - Women 2019
  • Medals: 6 (3 gold, 3 bronze)
    • Gold – 2022 (4 x 50m Medley Relay 20 Points - Mixed, 50m Butterfly S6 - Women); 2019 (100m Backstroke S7 - Women)
    • Bronze – 2023 (200m Individual Medley SM6 - Women, 50m Butterfly S6 - Women); 2022 (50m Freestyle S6 - Women)

bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Martin joined the Marine Corps after graduating high school and served three tours of duty in Iraq as a staff sergeant. He acquired two traumatic brain injuries in two separate incidents where his Humvee rolled over an improvised explosive device. He was awarded a Purple Heart after the first explosion, but it was the second explosion that resulted in more serious injuries for him - he even temporarily lost his ability to read and walk. He started cycling in 2011, and it didn’t take long for him to become one of the best riders in the country. In 2013, he won three national titles and set the American Record in the 4k track pursuit. His Paralympic journey started with the inaugural Warrior Games where he won the title of Ultimate Champion, the highest individual honor
  • Married to Tonya Martin
  • Has one sister, Kim
  • Hobbies include shooting, scuba diving, hanging out with my family and fostering pit bulls
  • He has four dogs and a cat.
Paralympic Experience
  • 1-time Paralympian
    • Paralympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, 4th (C4 - 4km Pursuit - Men), 5th (C4 - Time Trial - Men), 15th (C4-5 - 1000m Time Trial - Men), 19th (C4-5 - Road Race - Men)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2016 – 5th (C4 - 4km Pursuit - Men)
  • Years of participation: C4 - 4km Pursuit - Men 2014, 2016; C1-5 - Scratch Race - Men 2014; C4 - 1000m Time Trial - Men 2014; C4 - Road Race - Men 2013, 2014; C4 - Time Trial - Men 2014
  • Top finish: – 2014 (C1-5 - Scratch Race - Men)

  • Was in kindergarten when he got into his first racing chair but didn’t race competitively until his late teens
  • Aspires to pursue a career as a physician’s assistant after racing
  • Heroes include his parents, Stephen Hawking and U.S. teammate Amanda McGrory
  • Enjoys going to concerts in his free time
  • First man to win five individual world titles at a single IPC event

bio.fun_fact: Enjoys rowing, biking, camping and crossword puzzles
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Specialty: Para Nordic Skiing (Biathlon and Cross-Country) - Sitting Classification
  • Registered nurse and care manager
  • Parents are Roger and Sharon Martin; has one sibling, Gerrad Martin
  • Enjoys rowing, biking, camping and crossword puzzles
Biographical Information

Erin Martin's path to Paralympic competition began following a climbing accident in North Bend, Washington. While traversing a rock face before beginning a technical climb, she slipped and fell approximately 30 feet. The fall resulted in a T4 spinal cord injury that left her paralyzed from the mid-chest down, along with multiple fractures. Her background as a registered nurse provided perspective on her recovery, and she focused on what remained possible rather than what had changed.

Following her recovery, Martin returned to her position as a care manager at Seattle Children's Hospital. She initially took up adaptive rowing with the Seize The Oar club and riding a recumbent hand-pedaled bicycle before discovering para Nordic skiing in early 2019. She was introduced to the sport by her colleague and friend, Heather Galeotalanza, who had begun the sport after sustaining her own spinal cord injury in a rock climbing accident. Martin immediately connected with the liberating feeling of being outside and the physically demanding nature of the sport. Despite having limited cross-country skiing experience and none in a sit ski, she made rapid progress. 

Martin competed in her first national championships in January 2020 at Soldier Hollow, Utah, followed by her first World Cup event in Germany the following month. By January 2022, Martin earned third place at the U.S. Sit Ski National Championships in Bozeman, Montana, the performance that secured her qualification for the Paralympic Winter Games Beijing 2022. 

Paralympic Experience
  • 2-time Paralympian
    • Paralympic Games Milan 2026, 10th (12.5km Individual - Sitting - Women), 11th (4 x 2.5km Relay Open - Open, Sprint Pursuit - Sitting - Women), 14th (1km Sprint - Sitting - Women), 15th (10km - Sitting - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Beijing 2022, 15th (7.5km - Sitting - Women), 16th (1km Sprint - Sitting - Women)
World Championships Experience

GETTING TO KNOW MARTINEZ

Favorite Movie: Super Troopers

Favorite Meal: Lasagna

Favorite TV Show: Rick and Morty

Favorite Professional Sports Team: Angels and Eagles


Q&A WITH MARTINEZ

What does it mean for you to represent the United States? "It means a lot to represent the U.S. because it's something that not everyone gets to do so it's an honor to be able to put on the uniform and compete."

What do you most enjoy about softball? "Baseball can tend to be so tense that it becomes unenjoyable but when I'm on the softball field, I'm always loose and enjoying myself."

How did you get started playing softball? "My father played softball when he was younger and naturally my older brothers and I started to play."

Who were your softball role models growing up? "As a kid, I watched my oldest brother Anthony play a lot and he was one of the best players around when I was a kid. He was a great pitcher as well as a good hitter so I knew if I played softball, I could be like him."

Did you play any other sports growing up? "Baseball and football"

What non-softball sport would you like to try? "I always felt like I could be good at tennis."
